coredata.dc:title: Nanofluid heat transfer enhancement in microchannels: Investigating phases interactions using multiphase Eulerian model
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Generous, Muhammad M.
Abu-Nada, Eiyad
Alazzam, Anas
coredata.dc:description: This study employs a robust multiphase Eulerian model to mimic heat transfer enhancement using nanofluids in a circular microchannel. Unlike the Eulerian models reported in the literature, the current model incorporates phase interactions, and its accuracy is significantly improved by integrating collision viscosity, kinetic viscosity, and frictional viscosity. Comparative analysis against other models like mixture and discrete phase models demonstrates superior performance of the current model, particularly for solid particle volume fractions exceeding 1 %. The model evaluates water-based nanofluids (Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, SiO2) in circular microchannels. Ag NPs/water excels at low volume fractions and high Reynolds numbers, while MgO/water performs better at higher volume fractions. Moreover, the analysis of the Euler number indicates a decreasing trend with increasing Reynolds number across all nanofluids. However, no notable variation in the Euler number is observed when maintaining constant volume fraction and Reynolds number. Ultimately, the model is applied to obtain bulk fluid properties, such as viscosity and thermal conductivity, after suspending the nanoparticles. Subsequently, these properties were incorporated into the single-phase model. Utilizing these derived bulk properties was found to substantially enhance the accuracy of the single-phase model, demonstrating its reliability by yielding results within 13.52 % of experimental data.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Khalifa University 2024: Hybrid and Gold Journals (Prepaid Pot)
coredata.dcterms:subject: Eulerian model
Interphase momentum exchange
Interphase heat exchange
Bulk properties of nanofluids

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Significance of Eulerian model at elevated volume fractions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic diagram of the problem under study.
The mesh employed in the current study.
Comparison of (a) local Nusselt number values for the current study with Rea et al. [36], and (b) pressure drop with the data provided by Mahdavi et al. [12].

Comparison of local Nusselt number values for discrete phase [12], mixture [12], and Eulerian model (current model) at volume fractions of (a) 0.65 % of Al2O3, (b) 2.76 % of Al2O3, (c) 0.32 % ZrO2, and (d) 1.32 % of ZrO2.
Volume fraction contours for silver nanoparticles at a volume fraction of 5 %.
The comparison of the Nusselt number profiles at volume fraction 1 % for (a) Re = 100, (b) Re = 200, and (c) Re = 300 for six types of nanofluids considered.
The comparison of average Nusselt numbers at volume fractions of 1 %, 3 %, and 5 % and Reynolds numbers of (a) 100, (b) 200, and (c) 300 for six nanofluids was considered in this study.
The average Nuseelt numbers enhancement at 1 %, 3 %, and 5 % volume fraction for (a) Re = 100, (b) Re = 200, and (c) Re = 300 for six nanofluids considered in this study.
. The comparison of wall temperature profiles with increasing volume fraction from 1 % to 5 % at Reynolds number of 100 for (a) Ag NPs, (b) MgO, and (c) ZnO, (d) Al2O3, (e) ZrO2, and (f) SiO2.
Euler number against volume fraction for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids at fixed volume fraction of 1 %.
Curve fitting using power law for relative viscosity of (a) Ag NPs, (b) MgO, and (c) ZnO, (d) Al2O3, (e) ZrO2, and (f) SiO2.
Curve fitting using power law for relative thermal conductivity of (a) Ag NPs, (b) MgO, and (c) ZnO, (d) Al2O3, (e) ZrO2, and (f) SiO2.

(a) Enhancing single-phase model accuracy with current model-derived properties, (b) Comparing local Nusselt numbers between the Eulerian model and single-phase model using properties obtained from correlation developed in this work.
Mesh independence investigation for Ag NPs/water.

head.ce:title: Nanofluid heat transfer enhancement in microchannels: Investigating phases interactions using multiphase Eulerian model

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Khalifa University of Science and Technology, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Khalifa University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Mechanical Engineering, Khalifa University of Science and Technology, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: System on Chip Lab, Khalifa University of Science and Technology, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Khalifa University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSystem on Chip Lab, Khalifa University of Science and Technology, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study employs a robust multiphase Eulerian model to mimic heat transfer enhancement using nanofluids in a circular microchannel. Unlike the Eulerian models reported in the literature, the current model incorporates phase interactions, and its accuracy is significantly improved by integrating collision viscosity, kinetic viscosity, and frictional viscosity. Comparative analysis against other models like mixture and discrete phase models demonstrates superior performance of the current model, particularly for solid particle volume fractions exceeding 1 %. The model evaluates water-based nanofluids (Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, SiO2) in circular microchannels. Ag NPs/water excels at low volume fractions and high Reynolds numbers, while MgO/water performs better at higher volume fractions. Moreover, the analysis of the Euler number indicates a decreasing trend with increasing Reynolds number across all nanofluids. However, no notable variation in the Euler number is observed when maintaining constant volume fraction and Reynolds number. Ultimately, the model is applied to obtain bulk fluid properties, such as viscosity and thermal conductivity, after suspending the nanoparticles. Subsequently, these properties were incorporated into the single-phase model. Utilizing these derived bulk properties was found to substantially enhance the accuracy of the single-phase model, demonstrating its reliability by yielding results within 13.52 % of experimental data.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: second invariant of deviatroic stress tensor

Reynolds number,
ρ V D
μ

ce:section.ce:para: In modern electronics, there is a growing need for heat transfer fluids with enhanced thermal conductivity and intricate geometries that can optimize and streamline the heat transfer process to achieve higher levels of efficiency. This demand sparks extensive research efforts in the field of electronic cooling and the thermal management of electronic systems [1]. The current trajectory towards reducing the size of electronic chips requires an equal improvement in heat transfer efficiency. As a response to this issue, the utilization of nanofluids, which are fluids containing nanoparticles, has emerged as a viable approach to enhance the thermal conductivity of heat transfer fluids [2].

The modeling of nanofluids presents challenges due to their complex multiphase composition [3]. Researchers have utilized various methodologies to evaluate thermal behavior. The methodologies encompassed in this set include mixture models [4–7], discrete phase models [6,8–12], single-phase models [13–15], volume of fluid models [13], and models based on molecular dynamics [16]. At first, discrete-phase models were favored, but it became apparent that single-phase models were inadequate in accurately representing the authentic multiphase interactions between solid particles and liquid phases [13].

The fundamental principle underlying the multiphase mixture model pertains to the development of a tailored fluid that incorporates various characteristics, including viscosity, thermal conductivity, and heat capacity, of the composite [17]. Generally, these properties are primarily assessed based on volume averages, occasionally leading to slightly higher values than anticipated. The properties of these mixtures are subsequently utilized to solve conservation equations, which include the continuity equation for mass conservation, the momentum equation for momentum conservation, and the energy equation for energy conservation [18]. The approach being discussed bears a strong resemblance to a single-phase model. The main advantage of employing mixture models lies in their enhanced time efficiency and decreased computational resource demands [4].

The discrete phase model is advanced, incorporating conventional conservation equations to account for the fluid phase [6]. In contrast, the motion and behavior of solid particles are resolved using a Lagrangian approach [19], often referred to as the Eulerian-Lagrangian model [6]. The methodology employed in this study is characterized by its high computational requirements, as it necessitates the meticulous tracking of individual particles. Therefore, it is commonly advised to utilize this particular model for applications characterized by low-volume fractions to minimize the quantity of tracked particles and decrease computational intensity.

In contrast, the multiphase Eulerian model relies on the fundamental principle of separately considering conservation equations, precisely the continuity equation, momentum equation, and energy equation, for each phase [13]. As a result, this method requires a more significant amount of computational resources. Nevertheless, consideration of these factors becomes crucial when dealing with intricate phase interactions, such as drag force, lift force, virtual mass force, and interphase heat transfer. These elements can introduce complexity and challenge the model and its resolution.

Various authors have extensively employed the Eulerian model in nanofluid modeling in previous studies. In their study, Akbari et al. employed the methodology above to conduct simulations on nanofluids consisting of Al2O3 particles dispersed in water [13]. The investigation encompassed a spectrum of volume fractions, from a minimum of 0.5 % to a maximum of 2 %. In a similar vein, Kalteh et al. utilized the Eulerian model to simulate the behavior of copper/water nanofluids, examining volume fractions ranging from 0 % to 5 % [20]. In addition, the model proposed by Amiri et al. was utilized in the investigation of CuO/water nanofluids [21]. The study specifically examined a volume fraction of 5 % and implemented a constant temperature boundary condition in a backward-facing step scenario.

However, it is crucial to perform a thorough examination to compare the precision of the Eulerian model with both the mixture and discrete phase models at different volume fractions. Conducting such an investigation is of utmost importance in ascertaining the model that provides the most excellent precision for particular volume fraction values. This research endeavor is crucial for examining a widely held assumption among researchers, which posits that the solid phase (particles) constitutes a continuous phase. However, it is crucial to consider that this assumption may not be valid, mainly when dealing with lower-volume fractions.

Another significant challenge in nanofluid modeling is the determination of nanofluids' viscosity and thermal conductivity. Although several correlations are available for these properties, employing different correlations may result in inconsistent values for a nanofluid. Therefore, utilizing a multiphase Eulerian model is imperative to achieve accurate outcomes in this context. Moreover, this model needs to incorporate a comprehensive range of interactions between different phases, such as lift force, drag force, virtual mass force, interphase momentum transfer, and interphase heat exchange. This will enable the precise calculation of nanofluid viscosity and thermal conductivity.

The primary objective of this study is to incorporate phase interactions, specifically solid-fluid interactions, into the Eulerian model. Solid particles, while lacking inherent viscosity, influence the viscosity of the base fluid. To address this, the study introduces a novel approach that defines three viscosity components for the solid phases: frictional viscosity, kinetic viscosity, and collision-induced viscosity. The study aims to identify the specific range of volume fractions where the phase interaction-driven multiphase mixture model outperforms the mixture and discrete phase models. An innovative method is employed to extract fluid properties from the multiphase Eulerian model, which is then utilized in a single-phase model to enhance accuracy against experimental data. Furthermore, the improved model will be applied to study Nusselt numbers and temperature profiles. The investigation will also compute nanofluids' viscosity and thermal conductivity, using these values to refine the single-phase model further for improved alignment with experimental observations.

This study is centered on the simulation of a three-dimensional circular microchannel oriented along the x-axis, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The channel possesses specific dimensions, including an internal diameter (ID) of 0.5 mm and an overall length of 100 mm. A constant heat flux is uniformly applied to the outer surface of the tube. A parabolic velocity profile is employed for each phase for the inlet boundary condition. A pressure outlet boundary condition is introduced at the conduit's outlet, maintaining a zero gauge pressure. The inlet and initial temperature are set at 300 K, and the gravitational effect is considered in the negative y-direction. Within the framework of the phase-interaction multiphase Eulerian model, the investigation encompasses six types of nanofluids: (1) Ag NPs, (2) MgO, (3) ZnO, (4) Al2O3 NPs, (5) ZrO2, and (6) SiO2.

Several standard assumptions are used to apply the phase interaction-driven Eulerian model for nanofluid analysis. The first assumption considers nanoparticles as spherical in shape. This simplification is frequently employed to simplify the modeling process and uncover fundamental aspects of the fluid dynamics and physics inherent to the problem. Second, the nanofluid is assumed to be devoid of any chemical reactions that could potentially modify the properties of both the fluid and the nanoparticles.

As with any robust scientific model, our current model incorporates certain assumptions to streamline the analysis and enhance its applicability. These assumptions, while simplifying, maintain the model's accuracy of its predictions.

Firstly, it is assumed that the solid particles are spherical in shape [22]. Although real-world particles may deviate from a perfect sphere, this assumption allows us to apply well-established mathematical principles and achieve reliable results. This simplification is a common practice in modeling and has been validated in numerous studies for its effectiveness in representing particle behavior.

Secondly, the model assumes there is no thermal gradient within the particle [22,23]. While this assumption might have significant implications for large particles, it is not critical for our study, which focuses on nano-sized particles. By assuming uniform thermal conditions, we can accurately predict the system's thermal response, ensuring our findings are robust and widely applicable.

The primary components of the governing equations for the multiphase Eulerian model primarily encompass mass conservation, momentum conservation, and energy conservation. For each phase, the steady-state continuity is resolved as [20,22]:
(1)
∇ .

ϕ j
ρ j
V j

0

In this context, ϕ j represents volume fraction, ρ j signifies density, and V
j
denotes the flow velocity. The conservation of momentum is addressed by employing individual momentum equations for each phase. This approach accounts for various interphase interactions, including interphase force transfer, lift force, virtual mass force, and turbulent dispersion force. The steady-state momentum conservation is thus comprehensively expressed as [20]:
(2)

denotes the turbulent dispersion force, which is set to zero, given this scenario's low Reynolds number limitation. The external forces, such as electric or magnetic forces, are provided by

and are considered zero in this study. Interphase interaction force can be described in terms of velocity difference between primary and secondary phases as [13,18]:

Here, Kij represents the interphase exchange coefficient quantifying the part of momentum transfer from one object to the other during a collision. Also, i stand for secondary phase (particles) and j stand for the primary phase (base fluid). The exchange coefficient for fluid-solid interactions can be approximated as follows [13]:

In this equation, τs is particulate relaxation time, ds is particle diameter, and f is drag function. It is also important to note that Ksl = Kls . The relaxation time is the balance between the inertial of the particle and the viscous force exerted on the fluid that can be estimated as [13]:
(5)

Furthermore, the lift force is a significant force that acts due to the pressure difference between the top and bottom of the solid particle. A part of it is also generated due to the rotation of particles. The primary source of lift force acting on particles is attributed to the velocity gradient within the primary phase, and this phenomenon can be represented through the following model [13,18]:
(9)

In the given equation, Cl represents the lift coefficient, and ϕs denotes the volume fraction of solid particles. The lift coefficient can be determined by taking into account both the classical lift and vorticity-induced lift, which are contingent upon the particle Reynolds number and vorticity Reynolds number, as follows [25]:
(10)

The virtual mass force emerges when the discrete phase undergoes acceleration relative to the continuous phase, and this can be approximated using the material derivative of relative velocity in the form of the following expression [13]:
(13)

When the solid volume fraction is below its maximum permissible value, the solid pressure term is incorporated into the momentum equation for the granular phase.

The radial distribution function is a correction factor that adjusts the likelihood of collisions among granular particles as the solid phase density increases. Alternatively, this function can be interpreted as the nondimensional separation distance between spheres. The modeling of the radial distribution function for collisions between identical solid particles is conducted using the following empirical function [25]:
(14)
g o
=
[
1 −
(
ϕ s
ϕ
s , max
)
1 3
]
− 1

In the above equation, go is the radial distribution function, ϕs is the volume fraction of solid particles, and ϕs,max is maximum packing limit for solid particles. Calculating this solid pressure resolves the pressure gradient component in the momentum equation for the solid phase. It consists of both the kinetic and collision terms, delineated as follows [25]:
(15)
P s
=

In the above equation, es is the coefficient of restitution and Θs is the granular temperature of solid phase particles. A frictional viscosity component can be incorporated to address the viscous-plastic transition when solid-phase particles reach the maximum solid volume fraction. The shear viscosity comprises collision contributions, kinetic aspects, and the frictional component [4].
(16)
μ s

μ
f r

It is important to note that kinetic and frictional viscosity stem from the collision and movement of particles within the base fluid. Both viscosities hold greater relevance, especially in the realm of nanofluids. Furthermore, their importance intensifies with an increase in volume fraction, particle diameter, granular temperature, and particle density, as depicted by the Syamlal model [4] which is given as:
(17)

It's crucial to highlight that the frictional element holds minimal importance when the particle volume fraction is low. Nevertheless, its significance escalates substantially as the particle volume fraction approaches the maximum packing limit. Schaeffer's expression [4] is used for frictional viscosity, which is as follows:
(19)

P s

2 D
Here, Ps denotes solid pressure, θ represents the friction angle that is taken 30 o , and I 2D stands for the second invariant of the deviatoric stress tensor. The deviatoric stress is leftover stress after subtracting hydrostatic stress, which is defined as [26]:
(20)
σ

Here,

is deviatoric stress tensor and δ ij is kronecker delta function. The hydrostatic pressure is subtracted because it does not contribute to the plastic deformation. An energy equation represents the conservation of energy, and this equation also accounts for the modeling of interphase heat exchange between different phases, as follows [13]:
(21)

represents heat generation resulting from shear stress. Interphase energy transfer is predicated on the principle that energy transferred from one phase is received by the other phase, and this can be mathematically represented as follows [13]:

The particle Nusselt number is defined following the Ranz-Marshall model [13], which can be expressed as follows:

0.33

The Prandtl number is associated with the base fluid within the above equation, while the Reynolds number is contingent upon the particle diameter within the solid phase. This correlation remains applicable for values below a Reynolds number of 776 and a Prandtl number of 250. A summary of the properties for the various phases employed in this study is provided in Table 1 .

The no-slip boundary condition prescribes that both the velocity of the solid particles and the base fluid come to a complete stop at the microchannel inner walls. This condition can be mathematically represented as follows [4]:
(26)
V
s l i p
= 0

Meanwhile, at the tube's inlet, a parabolic velocity profile is specified as follows [4]:
(27)
V =
V
m a x
(

)
2
)

In the above equation, Vmax represents the maximum velocity in meters per second, R signifies the radius of the microchannel in meters, and r indicates the radius at a specific point in the radial direction. The pressure outlet boundary condition is implemented at the tube's outlet, signifying that the gauge pressure at this location is set to zero. This condition is expressed mathematically as follows [6]:
(28)
P
g a u g e
= 0

Furthermore, a constant inward heat flux is applied to the circumferential walls of the channel, as described below [4]:
(29)
q
″
=
q
″
o

Ensuring the robustness of simulation outcomes against variations in mesh size is of utmost importance. In our investigation, a mesh independence study was conducted for Ag NPs/water nanofluids at a volume fraction of 3 %, serving as a representative case. The study started with 110,525 polyhedral mesh elements, refined to 145,263 cells, and variations in average Nusselt number values are compared, as depicted in Table 2 . The findings demonstrate mesh independence when the polyhedral mesh elements exceed 140,000. Consequently, the current study utilizes a mesh comprising 145,263 polyhedral elements, confirming the results reliability. A visual representation of the mesh employed in this study is presented in Fig. 2 .

The present model's validation process involves a comparison with experimental data provided by Rea et al. [36] for two distinct cases: ϕ = 2.76 % Al2O3/water and ϕ =1.32 % volume fraction ZrO2/water. Local Nusselt number values are scrutinized along the duct's length at multiple locations. This scrutiny reveals a commendable concurrence in Nusselt number values against Rea et al. [36] for both scenarios, as visually depicted in Fig. 3 (a). The present model produces Nusselt number estimates within a range of ±2.03 % and ±1.02 % compared to the experimental data of Rea et al. [36]. Any disparities could stem from assumptions like spherical particles and uniform thermal gradients within the particles. Moreover, experimental inaccuracies might also contribute.

Furthermore, the dataset supplied by Mahdavi et al. [12] serves as a benchmark to assess the pressure drop validation for Al2O3 with a volume fraction of 2.76 %. In this assessment, the model demonstrates a robust alignment with pressure drop values across various Reynolds numbers, as exemplified in Fig. 3(b). The current concluded that the maximum pressure difference is estimated to be 4.35 % against Mahdavi et al. [12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Significance of Eulerian model at elevated volume fractions
ce:section.ce:para: The primary aim of this section is to conduct a comparative analysis of local Nusselt number values achieved by the current Eulerian model, contrasting them with results obtained from the mixture model and discrete phase models reported by Mahdavi et al. [12]. A thorough examination of the findings presented in Fig. 4 (a) and Fig. 4(b) unveils a nuanced scenario. When the volume fraction of Al2O3 is at 0.65 %, the mixture model and discrete phase model outperform the Eulerian model, indicating the latter's inferior accuracy. However, as the volume fraction of Al2O3 escalates to 2.76 % (as demonstrated in Fig. 4(b), the current model appears as the best choice compared to the other two models, signifying its superior suitability for this specific high-volume fraction scenario.

Similarly, this study extends the comparative analysis to all three models, focusing on their performance in the case of ZrO2 at volume fractions of 0.32 % and 1.32 %. A meticulous examination of the outcomes yields distinctive insights. When the volume fraction is at 0.32 % (as depicted in Fig. 4(c), the Eulerian model demonstrates a less favorable performance than the mixture model and the discrete phase models, again highlighting its limited accuracy in this specific context.

Conversely, when the volume fraction is raised to 1.32 % (as shown in Fig. 4(d)), the phase interaction-driven multiphase Eulerian model emerges as the superior choice. This demonstrates that the phase interaction-driven Eulerian model is well-suited for scenarios where the volume fraction surpasses 1 %. This conclusion underscores the model's applicability and efficacy in such conditions.

This section analyzes volume fraction, pressure, and velocity profiles derived from the conducted study using the Eulerian model. The examination of volume fraction contours for Ag NPs with a volume fraction of 5 % and a Reynolds number (Re) of 100 reveals sedimentation occurring at the lower region of the pipe, as visually depicted in Fig. 5 . This phenomenon is a result of the gravitational forces that act on the nanoparticles in the negative y-direction. Nevertheless, it is worth noting that the magnitude of sedimentation observed is relatively minor, attributed to the elevated Reynolds number, which mitigates sedimentation effects. It is worth mentioning that while we are presenting the findings in Fig. 5 for silver nanoparticles, similar observations were also made when using other types of nanoparticles.

The study presents a comprehensive examination of the local Nusselt number profiles along the axial dimension for a selection of nanomaterials, including Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2, dispersed within a water-based medium at a consistent volume fraction of 1 %. These profiles, displayed in Fig. 6 , provide insights into the heat transfer characteristics of the materials under investigation.

Within Fig. 6, the comparison of local Nusselt number profiles for Reynolds numbers of 100, 200, and 300 are displayed in Fig. 6(a), Fig. 6(b), and Fig. 6(c) respectively, while maintaining a constant volume fraction of 1 %. The results reveal that Ag NPs exhibit the highest local Nusselt number, with MgO, Al2O3, SiO2, ZnO, and ZrO2 demonstrating lower heat transfer performance when compared to Ag NPs.

An additional noteworthy observation pertains to the local Nusselt number profiles, which exhibit an upward trend as Reynolds numbers increase while keeping uniform volume fractions for each case. It is essential to emphasize that calculating the local Nusselt number at the wall surface is evaluated by performing a heat balance where the convection is equal to conduction at the surface. This calculation of the local Nusselt number is expressed mathematically as follows [4]:
(30)
N u
( x )
=
h
( x )
D
k
Here, h(x) is local heat transfer coefficient, k is effective thermal conductivity, and D is the diameter of the microchannel channel. The local heat transfer coefficient is estimated as [37]:
(31)
h
( x )
=
q "
T w
( x )
−
T f
( x )

The heat flux in the above expression can be replaced using the Fourier law of heat transfer as [37]:
(32)
q " = k
(
d T
d r
)

The local heat transfer coefficient can be modified using Fourier law as follows [37]:
(33)
h
( x )
=
k
T w
( x )
−
T f
( x )
(
d T
d r
)

The final expression used in the current study for Nusselt number calculations is obtained by substituting the expression for the local heat transfer coefficient in Eq. (30). It can be mathematically expressed as [22]:
(34)
N u
( x )
=
D
T w
( x )
−
T f
( x )
(
d T
d r
)

It is essential to compute the average Nusselt number for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 in conjunction with the base fluid, water. The average Nusselt numbers are computed as [4]:
(35)
N u
‾
=
∫ 0 L
N u
( x )
d x
L

Fig. 7 presents the average Nusselt numbers for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids at volume fractions of 1 %, 3 %, and 5 % and at a Reynolds number of 100. For the nanofluids mentioned above, the average Nusselt numbers are as follows: 5.54, 5.19, 4.89, 5.10, 4.77, and 4.89, respectively, for the 1 % volume fraction. Upon increasing the volume fraction to 3 % while maintaining the Reynolds number at 100, the average Nusselt numbers exhibit an upward trend, resulting in values of 5.68, 5.82, 5.27, 5.67, 4.98, and 5.22 for the same sequence of nanofluids. Further elevating the volume fraction to 5 % while keeping the Reynolds number constant yields average Nusselt numbers of 5.78, 6.31, 5.64, 6.15, 5.23, and 5.45, as visually depicted in Fig. 7(a).

In Fig. 7(b), we present the average Nusselt numbers for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids at volume fractions of 1 %, 3 %, and 5 %, while maintaining a Reynolds number of 200. For these nanofluids, the computed average Nusselt numbers are as follows: 6.04, 5.50, 5.14, 5.36, 5.00, and 5.10, respectively, for the 1 % volume fraction. Upon increasing the volume fraction to 3 %, with the Reynolds number held constant, the average Nusselt numbers show an ascending pattern, resulting in values of 6.22, 6.15, 5.54, 5.94, 5.20, and 5.40 for the same sequence of nanofluids. Further elevating the volume fraction to 5 % while maintaining a constant Reynolds number yields average Nusselt numbers of 6.33, 6.60, 5.89, 6.39, 5.42, and 5.69, as visually depicted in Fig. 7(b).

Within Fig. 7(c), the data highlights the average Nusselt numbers for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids, considering volume fractions of 1 %, 3 %, and 5 %, while sustaining a fixed Reynolds number of 300. For these nanofluids, the resulting average Nusselt numbers are 6.45, 5.85, 5.48, 5.70, 5.34, and 5.43, respectively, for the 1 % volume fraction. When the volume fraction is increased to 3 %, with the Reynolds number remaining constant, a clear pattern emerges, leading to average Nusselt numbers of 6.66, 6.49, 5.88, 6.27, 5.53, and 5.70 for the same sequence of nanofluids. Subsequently, raising the volume fraction to 5 % while maintaining the Reynolds number constant results in average Nusselt numbers of 6.78, 6.92, 6.21, 6.70, 5.73, and 5.97, as visually represented in Fig. 7(c).

The most widely employed and cost-effective cooling fluid is water. Given that this study focuses on nanofluids, it is crucial to assess the extent of heat transfer enhancement achieved in comparison to pure water. Consequently, Fig. 10 provides a comparison of heat transfer enhancement for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids at volume fractions of 1 %, 3 %, and 5 % in relation to pure water.

Fig. 8 (a) illustrates the average Nusselt number enhancements for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids at volume fractions of 1 %, 3 %, and 5 %, with a Reynolds number of 100, in comparison to pure water. For these nanofluids, the average Nusselt number enhancements are as follows: 21.56 %, 13.90 %, 7.27 %, 12.07 %, 4.78 %, and 7.43 %, respectively, for the 1 % volume fraction. Upon increasing the volume fraction to 3 %, while maintaining the Reynolds number at 100, the average Nusselt number enhancements exhibit an upward trend, resulting in values of 24.59 %, 27.76 %, 15.62 %, 24.38 %, 9.32 %, and 14.65 % for the same sequence of nanofluids. Further elevating the volume fraction to 5 %, while keeping the Reynolds number constant yields average Nusselt number enhancements of 26.80 %, 38.47 %, 23.80 %, 35.00 %, 14.75 %, and 19.64 %, respectively.

In Fig. 8(b), we depict the average Nusselt number enhancements for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids, each at volume fractions of 1 %, 3 %, and 5 %, all within a Reynolds number of 100, in comparison to pure water. For these nanofluids, the observed average Nusselt number enhancements are as follows: 26.16 %, 14.85 %, 7.29 %, 12.00 %, 4.47 %, and 6.53 %, respectively, for the 1 % volume fraction. As we progress to a 3 % volume fraction while keeping the Reynolds number constant at 100, the average Nusselt number enhancements demonstrate an ascending pattern, yielding values of 29.92 %, 28.39 %, 15.72 %, 24.18 %, 8.74 %, and 12.76 % for the same sequence of nanofluids. Subsequently, upon further elevating the volume fraction to 5 %, and maintaining the Reynolds number constant, we observe average Nusselt number enhancements of 32.28 %, 37.92 %, 23.05 %, 33.60 %, 13.14 %, and 18.91 %, respectively.

Fig. 8(c) presents the computed average Nusselt number enhancements for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids, each with volume fractions of 1 %, 3 %, and 5 %, all held at a Reynolds number of 100, in comparison to pure water. For these nanofluids, the calculated average Nusselt number enhancements are as follows: 28.44 %, 16.43 %, 9.06 %, 13.51 %, 6.26 %, and 8.05 %, respectively, for the 1 % volume fraction. Upon progressing to a 3 % volume fraction, while maintaining the Reynolds number constant at 100, the average Nusselt number enhancements reveal an ascending trend, resulting in values of 32.70 %, 29.19 %, 16.99 %, 24.92 %, 10.12 %, and 13.57 % for the same sequence of nanofluids. Subsequently, with a further increase in the volume fraction to 5 % while keeping the Reynolds number constant, we observe average Nusselt number enhancements of 34.92 %, 37.77 %, 23.72 %, 33.42 %, 14.07 %, and 18.95 %, respectively.

Numerous notable observations merit further discussion. Particularly interesting is the behavior of Ag/water and MgO/water nanofluids. At a 1 % volume fraction and a Reynolds number of 100, Ag NPs/water emerges as the leading nanofluid, as demonstrated in Fig. 8(a). However, as the volume fraction rises, its effectiveness decreases, eventually being surpassed by MgO/water.

Moreover, as the Reynolds number escalates to 200, Ag NPs/water maintains its superiority, even at a 3 % volume fraction. However, beyond this threshold, MgO emerges as the more efficient option. This phenomenon is attributable to the notably high density of silver nanoparticles (10,490 kg/m³), making them more prone to sedimentation, especially at low Reynolds numbers and high volume fractions, in contrast to the relatively lighter MgO particles. This behavior has been previously elucidated by Generous et al. [4]. Furthermore, it is worth noting that the primary factors influencing shear viscosity are kinetic viscosity and collision viscosity, both directly correlated with particle density. Consequently, higher volume fractions lead to a more pronounced increase in nanofluid viscosity, subsequently diminishing their heat transfer capabilities.

The cooling impact induced by Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids holds significant relevance for a range of applications, particularly in electronic cooling. Consequently, it is imperative to investigate wall temperature profiles. This study aims to examine the influence of volume fraction on wall temperature. Wall temperature profiles at Reynolds number of 100 for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids are visually represented in Fig. 9 (a) through Fig. 9(f), respectively.

Several significant observations warrant discussion. Firstly, the cooling effect seamlessly corresponds with the heat transfer hierarchy of the nanofluids investigated in this study. For example, the most thermally conductive nanofluid, Ag NPs/water, demonstrates the most efficient cooling effect and maintains the lowest temperature at a 1 % volume fraction, as depicted in Fig. 9(a).

A second noteworthy observation pertains to the substantial impact of increasing volume fraction from 1 % to 5 % on the cooling effect in all nanofluids, except for Ag NPs/water, where raising the volume fraction does not yield a significant advantage in terms of cooling efficacy. Thirdly, it is evident that the cooling effect is notably more pronounced in Ag NPs compared to the other fluids at a 1 % volume fraction. However, as the volume fraction increases to 5 %, MgO/water surpasses the cooling effect of Ag NPs.

As previously mentioned, among the analyzed nanofluids with low volume fractions, Ag NPs/water nanofluids demonstrate the highest cooling efficiency. This superior performance primarily arises from the remarkably high thermal conductivity of silver nanoparticles (429 W/m.K) in contrast to other particles examined in this study. However, as the volume fraction increases, the cooling efficiency of Ag NPs diminishes compared to other nanofluids. This decline is associated with the significantly high density of silver nanoparticles, leading to two main issues: increased sedimentation and a direct impact on particle shear viscosity (both kinetic and collision viscosities), which are directly related to particle density. Consequently, with the rise in volume fraction, the viscosity of Ag NPs/water nanofluids escalates more rapidly.

This study also compares Euler number which is the ratio of actual pressure drop to the dynamic pressure, that can be mathematically expressed as [38]:
(36)
E u =
Δ
P
1 2
ρ
V
a v g
2
Here, Eu is the Euler number, ΔP is pressure drop, and
1 2
ρ
V
a v g
2
represents dynamic pressure. In Fig. 10 (b), the Euler number is compared across various nanofluids, including Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids at a fixed volume fraction of 1 %. Upon examination, it is clear that the Euler number exhibits relative constancy in nanofluids when both volume fraction and Reynolds number are kept constant. This phenomenon arises because, with increased volume fraction, both pressure drop and dynamic pressure increase, resulting in a nearly constant ratio that constitutes the Euler number. However, as illustrated in Fig. 10, there is a consistent decline in the Euler number with an escalating Reynolds number. To be precise, the Euler numbers are ∼ 138.8, ∼ 67.4, and ∼ 44.9 at Reynolds numbers of 100, 200, and 300, respectively.

Additionally, it is important to highlight that water demonstrates a lower Euler number when compared to nanofluids across all specified Reynolds numbers. This is attributed to the lower pressure drop in the absence of solid nanoparticles. The Euler numbers for water are determined to be 128, 64, and 42.6 at Reynolds numbers of 100, 200, and 300, respectively.

Recovering Properties from the Eulerian Model and Their Implications Assessing properties like viscosity and thermal conductivity in multiphase fluids, such as nanofluids, presents a challenging endeavor due to the intricate interactions among multiple phases. While there exist several models for describing the viscosity and thermal conductivity of nanofluids in the literature, it is notable that when applied in conjunction with single-phase models, these models often fail to accurately predict heat transfer and pressure drop values. Therefore, this study employs the pressure drop for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids and extracts viscosity from them using the Hagen-Poiseuille relation [39]. The following relation is utilized to determine viscosity values [39]:
(37)
μ =
Δ
P
D
2
32
L
V
a v g
In the above equation, ΔP is a pressure drop in Pa, D is the diameter in meters, L is the length of the channel, and Vavg is the average velocity of the fluid in the channel.

Viscosity values are computed as detailed previously, and relative viscosity values are fitted using a power law for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids across volume fractions ranging from 1 % to 5 %. The resulting curves and power law equations for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids are presented in Fig. 11 (a) through Fig. 11(f), respectively. The viscosity data for all nanofluids displayed an excellent fit with high R-square values of ∼ 0.9999. Additionally, the sum of squares errors (SSE), indicating the quality of fits, were calculated as 6.435 ×10−6, 6.413 ×10−6, 6.251 ×10−6, 6.588 ×10−6, 6.271 ×10−6, and 6.588 ×10−6 for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids, respectively.

In a manner similar to viscosity calculations, thermal conductivities for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids are determined by average Nusselt number values computed before, as [4]:
(38)
k =
h ¯
D
N u
‾
Here, k is effective thermal conductivity, D is hydraulic diameter,
N u
‾
is average Nusselt number and
h ¯
is average heat transfer coefficient which is calculated as [4]:
(39)
h ¯
=
∫ 0 L
h
( x )
d x
L
Here, h(x) is local heat transfer coefficient which is estimated as expressed in Eq. (31). The relative thermal conductivity values are fit to a power-law model for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids across volume fractions ranging from 1 % to 5 %. The resulting curves and power-law equations for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids are displayed in Fig. 12 (a) through Fig. 12(f), respectively. The thermal conductivity data for all nanofluids displayed an excellent fit with high R-square values of ∼ 1. Additionally, SSE, indicating the goodness of fits, were calculated as 6.113 ×10−17, 2.264 ×10−9, 9.279 ×10−10, 2.093 ×10−9, 3.525 ×10−10, and 4.397 ×10−10 for Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2 nanofluids, respectively.

An essential observation lies in the significantly higher thermal conductivity of Ag NPs/water nanofluid than others. This notable thermal enhancement can be credited to the exceptional thermal conductivity of silver nanoparticles (429 W/m·K).

The obtained values for viscosity and thermal conductivity, derived from data curve fitting, are utilized to define a custom single-phase fluid for Al2O3/water. Subsequently, the local heat transfer coefficient is calculated. The hydraulic diameter and channel length are defined following the specifications set by the experiment of Akbari et al. [13]. In this process, single-phase model results and experimental data provided by Akbari et al. [13] are taken, and the results are compared with the single-phase model using properties from the current Eulerian model. The single-phase model employed for comparison utilized conservation equations (including the continuity equation, momentum equation, and energy equation) and incorporated mixture properties such as viscosity and thermal conductivity. These properties were calculated using correlations developed in this study. The comparison illustrates that employing viscosity and thermal conductivity from the phase-interaction-driven multiphase Eulerian model significantly enhances the accuracy of the results, as depicted in Fig. 13 (a). Notably, the maximum discrepancy with revised properties is 13.52 % when compared with the experimental data provided by Akbari et al. [13]. Furthermore, it is evident that the values of the heat transfer coefficient exhibit a considerable enhancement when compared to single-phase model data provided by Akbari et al. [13], which relies on the following correlation for viscosity and thermal conductivity [13]:
(40)
μ =
(
1 + 0.025 ϕ + 0.015
ϕ
2
)
μ f
(41)
k
n f
=
k f
(
1 − ϕ
)
+ γ
k s
ϕ +
C d
d f
d s
R
e s 2
P r ϕ
Here, kf and ks are thermal conductivities of the base fluid and solid particles, respectively; γ = 0.01; Cd = 18 × 10−6; Res and Pr is Reynolds number and Prandtl number, respectively.

We have also compared the local Nusselt number profiles of the current model with those of the single-phase model utilizing properties from the current multiphase model. Notably, Fig. 13(b) exhibits a robust agreement between the two, underscoring the importance of various interactions, encompassing momentum and energy exchange between phases, as well as variations in viscosity and thermal conductivity arising from intricate phase interactions. These factors are taken into account in the current model but typically disregarded in the conventional single-phase approach.

This study employs a robust multiphase Eulerian model, outperforming mixture and discrete phase models, particularly for solid particle volume fractions over 1 %. It enhances the single-phase model by integrating viscosity and thermal conductivity data from the proposed model, reducing discrepancies to within 13.52 % of experimental heat transfer values. Furthermore, it evaluates water-based nanofluids, including Ag NPs, MgO, ZnO, Al2O3, ZrO2, and SiO2, within circular microchannels. Ag NPs/water have high Nusselt number enhancement at low volume fractions and high Reynolds numbers, while MgO/water performs better at higher volume fractions. For instance, at 1 % volume fraction and Reynolds number of 100, Ag NPs/water (21.56 %) outperform MgO/water (13.90 %). However, at a 3 % volume fraction, MgO/water (27.76 %) surpasses Ag NPs/water (24.59 %). Additionally, ZrO2/water exhibits the least heat transfer enhancement. Furthermore, the examination of the Euler number has revealed a declining pattern with the rise in Reynolds number for all nanofluids. Additionally, no significant variance in the Euler number is observed when the volume fraction and Reynolds number are held constant.

Muhammad M. Generous: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Eiyad Abu-Nada: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Anas Alazzam: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Abu Dhabi's Advanced Technology Research Council through the ASPIRE research award (Grant No. AARE20–358).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Eulerian-Eulerian two-phase numerical simulation of nanofluid laminar forced convection in a microchannel
